medications.indication_rationale: Reduces the Malassezia yeast load that drives seborrhoeic dermatitis. Applied to the scalp and face, lathered and left on for 5 minutes before rinsing, every 2-3 days (as per the source note), then continued long-term at a lower frequency to prevent relapse.
medications.dose_route_frequency: ketoconazole 2% shampoo, lather on scalp and face and leave 5 minutes before rinsing, every 2-3 days (as per source note)
contraindications_in_this_case: Avoid getting shampoo into the eyes; rinse thoroughly - it is a scalp/skin antifungal, not an ocular preparation

medications.indication_rationale: A short course of mild topical steroid applied sparingly to the facial plaques twice daily for a maximum of 5-7 days (as per the source note) rapidly settles the facial inflammation, itch and burning. A mild agent and a short course are appropriate for delicate facial skin.
medications.dose_route_frequency: hydrocortisone 1% cream, apply sparingly to the facial plaques twice daily for 5 days (maximum 5-7 days on the face), as per source note
contraindications_in_this_case: Do NOT apply to the eyelids or get it in the eye - ocular steroid exposure risks raised eye pressure/glaucoma and can worsen an unrecognised herpetic keratitis
Do not use long-term on the face - prolonged facial steroid causes skin atrophy, telangiectasia and perioral/steroid dermatitis

medications.indication_rationale: Seborrhoeic dermatitis with anterior blepharitis is a benign yeast-driven inflammatory condition, not a bacterial infection. Systemic antibiotics are not indicated in an afebrile patient with a negative periorbital-cellulitis screen; prescribing them is a key error of commission this case tests. Antibiotics are reserved only for a genuine secondary periorbital cellulitis (see safety-net).

No features of herpetic infection (no grouped vesicles, no punched-out erosions, no true eye pain/photophobia/vision change). Antivirals are not indicated for benign seborrhoeic dermatitis and should only be considered if eczema herpeticum or herpetic keratitis declares itself (see safety-net).

A potent steroid is not indicated on facial or periocular skin; a mild agent (hydrocortisone 1%) for a short course is sufficient and far safer. Potent steroids on the face risk atrophy and, near the eye, ocular complications.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Perform gentle mechanical eyelid hygiene twice daily: soak a clean cotton bud in diluted baby shampoo (or warm water) and gently wipe along the lid margins to lift the greasy crusts off the lash bases.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Physically removing the yeast and oil debris at the lash line relieves the burning, stops the eyes gluing shut, and treats the anterior blepharitis - the mainstay of lid-margin control, done by the patient at home.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Do not pick or scratch the greasy scales on the face and scalp; let the medicated shampoo and cream do the work.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Scratching raw skin risks introducing a secondary bacterial infection and worsens the inflammation.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Acknowledge the severe psychosocial impact, reassure that the flare typically improves within 3-5 days of treatment, and offer GP follow-up for mood and for the impact on work if low mood persists.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The condition is causing housebound distress and low mood; validating this and setting realistic expectations is part of good care and supports the acute psychosocial crisis.

patient_education_points.point: This is a common, benign, long-term relapsing skin condition driven by a normal skin yeast overgrowing when you are stressed or the weather is cold and dry. It can be well controlled but not permanently cured.
The medicated shampoo lowers the yeast on the scalp and face, and the mild steroid cream calms the redness and itch - the extreme itch and facial redness usually improve noticeably within 3-5 days.
Only use the steroid cream on the facial skin for a maximum of 5-7 days and keep it well away from the eyes and eyelids; keep using the medicated shampoo long-term to prevent relapses.
Clean the eyelid crusts gently with diluted baby shampoo on a cotton bud each morning and night to relieve the burning and stop the eyes sticking shut.
Watch the eyes closely: any change in your actual vision, severe pain inside the eye, pain when looking at light, or spreading hot swelling around the eye needs emergency eye assessment.

follow_up_plan.interval: prompt in-person GP review within 24-48 hours to examine the periocular region, then routine review in about 7 days to assess resolution and transition to maintenance
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Resolution of the blepharitis and facial flare, safety of the eye/cornea on in-person examination, transition off the topical steroid to long-term maintenance shampoo, and the patient's mood and return to work.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any vision change, severe deep eye pain, pain on looking at light, spreading hot swelling around the eye, fever, or grouped blisters / punched-out sores - seek urgent same-day in-person / ED care, do not wait for the review.

must_include_items: Recognise a benign, common, chronic-relapsing greasy-scale facial/scalp eruption with associated anterior lid-margin crusting rather than an acute eye infection
Screen for and safety-net the sight-threat / periocular mimics (herpetic keratitis, periorbital/orbital cellulitis, eczema herpeticum)
Recommend ketoconazole 2% shampoo to scalp and face and a short (max 5-7 day) course of mild hydrocortisone 1% to the facial plaques, kept away from the eyes
Advise gentle mechanical eyelid hygiene (diluted baby shampoo on a cotton bud) twice daily
Explicitly advise AGAINST systemic antibiotics and antivirals for this benign eruption and against putting steroid on the eyelids
Offer a prompt (24-48 h) in-person eye look given telehealth cannot examine the eye, and give a clear mandatory eye safety-net
acceptable_alternatives: Arranging same-day in-person / optometry / GP review specifically to examine the eye is acceptable and appropriately cautious given the periocular involvement
Adding a bland emollient or coal-tar / other antifungal shampoo (e.g. selenium sulfide, zinc pyrithione, ciclopirox) as an alternative to ketoconazole
Referral for psychological support if low mood persists after the skin settles
errors_of_omission: Failing to give an explicit eye safety-net for the sight-threatening periocular mimics
Failing to screen for eye red flags (vision change, deep eye pain, photophobia, spreading hot swelling, fever, vesicles) before reassuring
Failing to warn to keep the topical steroid away from the eyes and to limit it to a short course
errors_of_commission: Escalating an afebrile patient with a negative sight-threat red-flag screen to the emergency department or calling an ambulance (over-triage) - the primary scored defect
Prescribing systemic antibiotics or antivirals for the benign eruption
Recommending a potent topical steroid on facial/periocular skin, or applying any steroid to the eyelids / into the eye
Dismissing the eyelid symptoms as 'just dandruff' with no eye screen or safety-net (under-triage)
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES (1) recognising the benign eruption and NOT escalating to ED / prescribing systemic antibiotics or antivirals for the afebrile, sight-threat-screen-negative presentation, and (2) an explicit, mandatory eye safety-net for the periocular mimics plus advice to keep steroid away from the eyes. Over-triage to ED/000, or antibiotic/antiviral prescription, is the primary scored defect; the opposite failure - dismissing the eye symptoms with no screen or safety-net - is a critical under-triage.
